text_blocks.text: When the news arrived in England it
caused an outcry. In response, a combined
bounty of PS1,000 was offered for Every's
capture by the Privy Council and East India
Company, leading to the first worldwide
manhunt in recorded history. The plunder of
Aurangzeb's treasure ship had serious
consequences for the English East India
Company. The furious Mughal Emperor
Aurangzeb ordered Sidi Yaqub and Nawab
Daud Khan to attack and close four of the
company's factories in India and imprison
their officers, who were almost lynched by a
mob of angry Mughals, blaming them for their
countryman's depredations, and threatened to
put an end to all English trading in India. To
appease Emperor Aurangzeb and particularly
his Grand Vizier Asad Khan, Parliament
exempted Every from all of the Acts of
Grace (pardons) and annesties it would
subsequently issue to other pirates.
